sources.notes: Find the minimum total weight of n-1 selected edges that do not form a tree.
Writeup reduces the answer to an MST plus one or two non-tree edges and uses tree path queries.

annotation.summary: Given a weighted simple graph, choose n-1 edges of minimum total weight whose selected subgraph is not a tree.
annotation.constraints: Sum of n,m is 2e5, so the solution must sort edges once and answer tree path maximum queries efficiently.
annotation.core_idea: Start from the minimum spanning tree. A non-tree set of n-1 edges can be viewed as adding one non-tree edge and deleting a suitable tree edge from the cycle, or in the special two-non-tree-edge case deleting the two largest relevant tree edges. Kruskal gives the baseline MST; HLD/DFS order plus range maximum structures check the best removable tree edge on or outside the involved paths.
annotation.complexity: O(m log m) with sorting and path/range maximum queries.
tricks: one or two non-tree edges are sufficient

solution_variants.name: mst-exchange-with-path-queries
solution_variants.summary: Build an MST, then evaluate adding non-tree edges and removing maximum eligible tree edges with HLD/RMQ.

tags.evidence: The solution begins from the MST and uses exchange arguments around non-tree edges.
tags.description: Minimum spanning tree construction and exchange/cycle arguments.
tags.created_reason: MST is a major graph retrieval category not covered by the broad graph tag.

tags.evidence: The implementation queries maxima on tree paths and path complements after rooting the MST.
tags.description: Decompose a tree into chains to answer path queries or updates efficiently.
tags.created_reason: Tree path-query problems should be searchable separately from generic tree DFS.

tags.evidence: Kruskal and the exchange step choose cheapest edges and remove maximum-weight replaceable edges.
Efficient range maximum/path maximum data structures are needed after building the MST.
